learningGoal: How to choose the right homophone (a word that sounds the same but has a different meaning) — this one trips up loads of people!
A quick meaning test that makes picking the correct spelling easy

screens.title: Can you spot the wrong word?
screens.body: Aisha is writing a story for English homework and wrote: **"Their going to the park after school."** Something looks wrong! Did you know that homophones are sneaky words that **sound the same** but have **different spellings and meanings**? Even adults get caught out by these!

visualProps.text: Their going to the park after school.
visualProps.label: Spot the mistake:

screens.title: Use the meaning test
screens.body: The wrong word is **Their**. It should be **They're**. 'They're' is short for 'they are'. 'Their' means belonging to them. Here's a handy test that'll help you get it right every time:

interaction.correctFeedback: You've nailed it! Read, think about meaning, then pick the right spelling. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly there — start by reading the sentence, then think about meaning.

steps.text: Read the sentence and find the homophone
steps.why: Look for words that could be confused

steps.text: Ask: what does the word MEAN here?
steps.why: Can you replace the word with 'they are'? If yes, use they're.
'They're' is short for 'they are'. 'Their' means belonging to them.

steps.text: Corrected: "They're going to the park after school."
steps.why: Now the meaning matches the spelling ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Right, you've got this! Pick the word that fits the sentence.

interaction.question: Which word correctly completes: "The children packed ___ bags for the trip."
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The bags belong to the children, so we need 'their' (belonging to them). ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The bags belong to the children, so we need 'their' (belonging to them).

visualProps.text: The children packed ___ bags for the trip.
visualProps.label: Fill in the correct word:

screens.title: Homophone recipe
screens.body: You've learned the secret weapon! When you see a word that sounds like another word, use the **meaning test** to pick the right spelling. This trick works every single time.

steps.why: What does the word actually mean here?
Their coats, their bags, their house
They're going, they're happy
Over there, there is, there are

steps.text: its = belonging to it / it's = it is
steps.why: Remember: the apostrophe means 'it is' ✓

learningGoal: How to spot homophone (same-sounding word) errors in writing — you'll become a mistake detective!
How to use meaning to choose the right spelling every time

screens.title: Can you spot Ella's mistake?
screens.body: Ella wrote a diary entry and wrote: **"We went over their to play in the garden."** One of the homophones is wrong — can you be the detective and find it?

visualProps.text: We went over their to play in the garden.
visualProps.label: Spot the mistake:

screens.title: Here's the mistake
screens.body: Ella wrote **"their"** but it should be **"there"**. Don't worry — this is one of the most common mix-ups! 'Their' means belonging to them. Here we mean a place, so it should be 'there'.

steps.text: ✗ "We went over their to play in the garden."
steps.why: "their" is the wrong homophone
'Their' means belonging to them. Here we mean a place, so it should be 'there'.

steps.text: ✓ "We went over there to play in the garden."
steps.why: Now the spelling matches the meaning ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: You've got this! Which word correctly fills the gap?

interaction.question: Which word correctly completes: "___ the best friend I've ever had!"
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'You're' is short for 'you are'. 'Your' means belonging to you. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! 'You're' is short for 'you are'. 'Your' means belonging to you.

visualProps.text: ___ the best friend I've ever had!
visualProps.label: Fill in the correct word:

screens.title: How to catch homophone mistakes
screens.body: Here's your superpower: always ask **what does this word mean in the sentence?** If the meaning doesn't match the spelling, swap it for the right homophone. You'll catch mistakes that most people miss!

steps.why: Your brain loves to auto-correct — don't let it trick you!
Words that sound alike but mean different things

steps.text: Ask: what does the word MEAN here?
steps.why: Meaning tells you the spelling
Spelling must match meaning, not sound ✓

learningGoal: How to remember which words have silent letters — they're sneakier than you'd think!
How to spot the common silent letter patterns (kn, wr, mb, ps) so they never catch you out

screens.title: The letter you can't hear!
screens.body: Did you know some letters in English are completely invisible to your ears? Finn is learning words with a silent K. Say the word **"knight"** out loud. Can you hear the **K**? No — because it's **silent**! But you still have to write it.

visualProps.text: "knight" sounds like "night" — the K is silent!
visualProps.label: Listen and look:

screens.title: The "kn-" pattern
screens.body: The word **"knight"** sounds like "night" because the **K** is silent. When a word starts with 'kn', the K is silent — you only say the N sound. Once you know this pattern, you can spell a whole family of words — here they are:

steps.why: When a word starts with 'kn', the K is silent — you only say the N sound.

steps.text: Common words: knife, knight, knot, knee, knock, know
steps.why: Learn these as a group — once you spot the pattern, you've got them all!

steps.text: Memory trick: picture the silent letter wearing an invisible cloak!
steps.why: It's there in the spelling, just hiding from your ears

steps.text: Say it with the silent letter: "knight" → k-night
steps.why: Saying the silent letter while you practise helps you remember it ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Time to put your new knowledge to the test! Which of these is spelled correctly?

interaction.question: Which spelling is correct?
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! The K in 'knuckle' is silent. We write 'kn' but only say the N sound. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The K in 'knuckle' is silent. We write 'kn' but only say the N sound.

screens.title: Silent letter patterns to remember
screens.body: Silent letters are tricky because you can't hear them — but here's the good news: they follow patterns! Learn the pattern and you'll get the whole group right.

steps.text: kn- → silent K: knife, knight, knot, knee, knock
steps.why: Say 'k-nife' in your head to remember the K

steps.text: wr- → silent W: write, wrong, wrap, wrist
steps.why: Say 'w-rite' in your head to remember the W

steps.text: -mb → silent B: climb, lamb, thumb, crumb
steps.why: Say 'clim-b' in your head to remember the B
Say 'p-sychology' in your head to remember the P ✓

learningGoal: How to spot when a silent letter has been missed out — a really common mistake!
How to use patterns to remember those invisible letters

screens.title: Can you spot Jake's mistake?
screens.body: Jake wrote in his spelling test and wrote **"nife"**. Hmm, a letter is hiding! Can you work out which one has gone missing?

screens.title: The silent letter was missing!
screens.body: Jake forgot the silent K. Words starting with the 'n' sound often need 'kn' at the start. The correct spelling is **"knife"** — the silent **K** follows the **"kn-"** pattern. This catches out loads of people, so knowing the pattern puts you ahead!

interaction.sentence: When a word starts with "kn-", the ____ is silent
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! The K is silent in the "kn-" pattern. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — in "kn-" words, the K is the silent letter!

steps.why: Jake forgot the silent K. Words starting with the 'n' sound often need 'kn' at the start.
The K is silent but always written
The correct spelling includes the silent K ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Now you try! Someone wrote **"rong"**. What should it be?

interaction.question: What is the correct spelling of "rong"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'Wrong' starts with 'wr' — the W is silent but must be written. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! 'Wrong' starts with 'wr' — the W is silent but must be written.

screens.title: Don't forget the silent letters!
screens.body: The most common mistake is leaving out letters you can't hear. But now you know the trick: **say the silent letter in your head** when you practise spelling the word. You've got this!

steps.text: If it sounds like 'n' at the start, check: is it 'kn'?
steps.why: knife, knight, knee, knock, know

steps.text: If it sounds like 'r' at the start, check: is it 'wr'?
steps.why: write, wrong, wrap, wrist

steps.text: If it sounds like 'm' at the end, check: is it 'mb'?
steps.why: climb, lamb, thumb, crumb

steps.text: Say the silent letter out loud when practising
steps.why: K-nife, w-rite, clim-b — it helps you remember! ✓

learningGoal: When to double a consonant (a non-vowel letter) before adding a suffix (an ending like -ing or -ed) — there's a satisfying rule for this!
How to apply the short vowel rule for doubling letters so you always get it right

screens.title: Run + ing = running or runing?
screens.body: Here's a spelling puzzle that catches out almost everyone! Hassan is adding -ing to words. When you add **-ing** to **"run"**, do you double the last letter? Let's crack it!

screens.title: The short vowel doubling rule
screens.body: Is it **"running"** or **"runing"**? Good news — there's a really satisfying rule that tells you exactly when to double the last letter:

interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! Check the word, check the vowel, check the consonant, then double. You've got it! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly there — start by looking at the base word first.

steps.text: Step 1: Look at the base word "run"
steps.why: Check the vowel (a, e, i, o, u) before the last letter

steps.text: Step 2a: SHORT vowels make a quick sound
steps.why: cAt, bEd, sIt, hOp, rUn — the vowel is quick and snappy

steps.text: Step 2b: LONG vowels say their own name
steps.why: cAke, thEme, rIde, hOpe, cUte — the vowel sounds like its letter name
It sounds like the "u" in "run" — quick and short!

steps.text: Step 3: Does it end with just ONE consonant?
steps.why: "run" ends with one "n" — yes!

steps.text: Step 4: Short vowel + one consonant = DOUBLE IT
steps.why: run → runn + ing = **running**

steps.text: Why? Without doubling, "runing" would change the vowel to a LONG sound
steps.why: "runing" looks like it should rhyme with "tuning" ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Let's see if you can use the rule! What happens when you add **-ing** to **"sit"**?

interaction.question: What is "sit" + "-ing"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Sit' has a short vowel (i) then a single consonant (t), so we double the T before adding -ing. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 'Sit' has a short vowel (i) then a single consonant (t), so we double the T before adding -ing.

screens.title: When to double the last letter
screens.body: Here's the golden rule to take away: **short vowel + single consonant = double it** before adding a suffix like -ing, -ed or -er. Once you know this, you'll spot the answer instantly!

steps.text: Check the vowel — is it short? (a, e, i, o, u as in cat, bed, sit, hop, run)
steps.why: Long vowels (like in 'hope') do NOT double

steps.text: Check the ending — is it a single consonant?
steps.why: Two consonants (like 'jump') do NOT double

steps.text: If both yes → double the consonant before the suffix
steps.why: run → running, hop → hopped, big → bigger

steps.text: If the vowel is long → DON'T double
steps.why: hope → hoping (not hopping!) ✓

learningGoal: How to spot when a letter should or shouldn't be doubled — these mistakes are everywhere!
How to avoid common double-letter mistakes that trip up even grown-ups

screens.title: Can you spot Aisha's mistake?
screens.body: Aisha wrote in her story and spelled a word as **"begining"**. Something's off with the double letters! This one catches out grown-ups too — can you spot it?

bodyParts.content: Aisha wrote **"begining"** — but the correct spelling is **"beginning"**.
The N needs to be doubled. Say 'begin' — the last part sounds like 'GIN' with a short vowel, so double the N before adding -ing.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Your turn to be the teacher! Someone wrote **"occured"**. What's the correct spelling?

interaction.question: What is the correct spelling?
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! 'Occur' has stress on the last part (oc-CUR), short vowel + single consonant, so double the R. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 'Occur' has stress on the last part (oc-CUR), short vowel + single consonant, so double the R.

screens.title: Double letter dos and don'ts
screens.body: Some words double letters, some don't — and now you know how to tell the difference! Use the short vowel rule for adding suffixes, and learn the tricky ones by heart.

steps.text: Short vowel + single consonant → double before a suffix
steps.why: running, hopped, bigger
hoping (not hopping), writing (not writting)

steps.text: Tricky doubles to learn: accommodate, beginning, occurred
steps.why: These follow the rule but people often forget

steps.text: Tricky singles to learn: until, tomorrow, necessary
steps.why: These are exceptions — just learn them by heart ✓

learningGoal: How to apply the famous 'i before e except after c' rule — one of the most useful spelling rules there is!
How to remember the sneaky exceptions that break the rule

screens.title: i before e — or e before i?
screens.body: Did you know there's a famous rhyme that's helped people spell for hundreds of years? Ella is learning the ie/ei pattern. Is it "bel-ie-ve" or "bel-ei-ve"? Let's learn the rule!

visualProps.text: i before e, except after c
visualProps.label: The golden rule:

screens.title: The rule and how to use it
screens.body: So is it **"believe"** with "ie" — or the other way round? The answer comes from this famous rhyme: **i before e, except after c** (when the sound is "ee"). It's been helping people spell for ages — let's see how it works:

interaction.sentence: The rule is: i before e, except after ____
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! "i before e, except after c" — that's the golden rule. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — the rule is "i before e, except after C"!

steps.why: believe, piece, field, thief, chief
receive, ceiling, deceive, receipt, conceive

steps.text: Check: is there a C right before?
steps.why: If yes → ei. If no → ie

steps.text: Careful! Some rebel words break the rule
steps.why: weird, seize, their, neither, protein — learn these rebels by heart ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Now it's your turn! Which of these is spelled correctly?

interaction.question: Which spelling is correct?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! No C before the sound, so it's i before e: ach-ie-ve. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! No C before the sound, so it's i before e: ach-ie-ve.

screens.title: i before e — the full picture
screens.body: The rule works brilliantly most of the time — just watch out for a few rebel words that like to break it!

steps.text: No C before → i before e (ie)
steps.why: believe, piece, field, thief, chief
receive, ceiling, deceive, receipt
These are the rebels — learn them separately
Scientific words often go their own way too ✓

learningGoal: How to spot ie/ei mistakes — they're some of the most common spelling errors around!
How to use the 'after c' part of the rule like a pro

screens.title: Can you spot Finn's mistake?
screens.body: Finn wrote in his English book and spelled a word as **"recieve"**. The ie/ei is the wrong way round! These two little letters cause more spelling headaches than almost anything else. Can you fix it?

screens.title: The ie/ei is swapped!
screens.body:
Finn wrote 'ie' after C, but after C it should be 'ei'. Receive → rec-ei-ve.

**Your ie/ei checklist:**
1. Is there a C right before? → use **ei** (receive, ceiling)
2. No C? Then usually → **ie** (believe, piece, field)
3. Check — is it an exception? (weird, seize, their, protein)
4. When in doubt, write both and see which looks right!

steps.why: The i and e are the wrong way round

steps.text: Finn wrote 'ie' after C, but after C it should be 'ei'. Receive → rec-ei-ve.
steps.why: Apply the rule: i before e, except after c
Now the ie/ei is correct ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Now you try! Someone wrote **"decieve"**. What's the correct spelling?

interaction.question: What is the correct spelling?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! There's a C before the sound, so it's 'ei' not 'ie': dec-ei-ve. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! There's a C before the sound, so it's 'ei' not 'ie': dec-ei-ve.

screens.title: ie or ei — your checklist
screens.body: You've now got a handy checklist that works almost every time. Here it is:

steps.text: Step 1: Is there a C just before?
steps.why: If YES → use EI (e.g. receive, ceiling, deceit)
If NO → use IE (e.g. believe, field, thief)
weird, seize, neither, their — these break the rule

steps.text: Memory trick: I before E, except after C
steps.why: Learn the exceptions separately ✓

learningGoal: Why the suffix (word ending) -ful always has one L, not two — this surprises almost everyone!
How to add -ful to base words correctly every single time

screens.title: -ful or -full?
screens.body: Here's a question that tricks nearly everyone! Grace is writing about her weekend and needs to write the word **"hopeful"**. But wait — is it one L or two? The word "full" has two Ls, so should the suffix?

screens.title: The -ful rule: always ONE L
screens.body: The answer is **"hopeful"** — with just ONE L. When "full" becomes a **suffix** (letters added to the end of another word), it loses one L and becomes **-ful**. Every single time!

steps.text: The word "full" on its own has two Ls
steps.why: The glass is full

steps.text: As a suffix, it drops to one L: -ful
steps.why: This ALWAYS happens — no exceptions!
NOT "hopefull" — just one L

steps.text: More examples: helpful, cheerful, powerful, playful
steps.why: Every -ful word follows this rule ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Let's see if you remember the rule! What do you get when you add -ful to **"care"**?

interaction.question: What is "care" + "-ful"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! The suffix -ful always has ONE L. Care + ful = careful. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The suffix -ful always has ONE L. Care + ful = careful.

screens.title: The -ful rule
screens.body: This is one of the simplest spelling rules — and one of the most commonly broken, even by adults! Now you know better.

steps.text: The word 'full' on its own = two Ls
steps.why: That's fine — it's a standalone word
helpful, beautiful, wonderful, thankful, hopeful
Every -ful word has one L at the end
The Y changes to I before adding -ful ✓

learningGoal: How to spot the sneaky -full error that pops up everywhere
Why -ful always has one L as a suffix (word ending) — a fact that puts you ahead of most people!

screens.title: Can you spot Jake's mistake?
screens.body: Jake wrote in his homework book and wrote **"wonderfull"**. Something's not right at the end of the word! Can you spot what's gone wrong?

screens.title: Too many Ls!
screens.body: Jake wrote -full with two Ls. As a suffix, -ful always has just one L.

steps.why: The suffix -ful never has two Ls

steps.text: "Full" drops an L when it becomes a suffix
steps.why: full → -ful (always!)
That's the correct spelling ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Your turn! Someone wrote **"powerfull"**. Can you fix it?

interaction.question: What is the correct spelling?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! The suffix -ful always has ONE L: powerful. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! The suffix -ful always has ONE L: powerful.

screens.title: One L for -ful — always!
screens.body: This is a guaranteed rule with zero exceptions — how satisfying is that? If it's a suffix, it's one L. Every time. No tricks!

steps.text: The suffix -ful ALWAYS has one L
steps.why: helpful, beautiful, wonderful, grateful

steps.text: The word 'full' on its own has two Ls
steps.why: Only the standalone word keeps both Ls

steps.text: No exceptions — this rule is 100% reliable
steps.why: If you see -full at the end, it's wrong

steps.text: Quick check: cover the -ful. Is there a word left?
steps.why: hope-ful, care-ful, play-ful — yes! One L ✓

learningGoal: How to choose between confusing suffix (word ending) pairs like -tion and -sion — they sound almost identical!
How to use clever memory tricks for -ent/-ant and -ence/-ance so you never mix them up

screens.title: Which ending is right?
screens.body: Did you know that even teachers sometimes have to double-check these? Aisha is learning -tion and -sion endings. Is it **-tion** or **-sion**? These confusing pairs trip up everyone — but you're about to learn the secret!

screens.title: -tion or -sion?
screens.body: These two endings sound almost the same — but only one is right! Here's how to tell them apart:

bodyParts.content: These two endings sound almost the same — but only one is right!

steps.why: Say it: "education" — hear the ending!
Say it: "television" — sounds different!
If the letter before the ending is L, N, R or S, use -sion (expuLsion, teNsion, veRsion, expresSion). Otherwise, use -tion!

steps.text: Still stuck? Think of a word you already know how to spell
steps.why: Words from the same family use the same ending ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: You've got this! Test yourself on **-tion** / **-sion**. Which spelling is correct?

interaction.question: Which spelling is correct?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The base word 'invent' ends in T, not L/N/R/S, so it's -tion: invention. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The base word 'invent' ends in T, not L/N/R/S, so it's -tion: invention.

screens.title: Suffix pairs cheat sheet
screens.body: These pairs are genuinely hard — even adults mix them up all the time. But now you've got the patterns, you're ahead of the game!

steps.text: -tion vs -sion: after L, N, R, S → use -sion
steps.why: expulsion, tension, version, expression
education, invention, action, station
Learn these as a group
Learn these as a separate group ✓

learningGoal: How to spot wrong suffix (word ending) pairs — become a spelling detective!
How to use related words as clues to check the suffix

screens.title: Can you spot Ben's mistake?
screens.body: Ben wrote in his geography book and wrote **"educasion"**. The suffix is wrong! Can you see which part?

screens.title: Wrong suffix!
screens.body: Ben used -sion, but 'educate' ends in T, so it's -tion: education.

steps.why: Wrong suffix used

steps.text: Ben used -sion, but 'educate' ends in T, so it's -tion: education.
steps.why: Think about the base word or the matching adjective/noun
Correct suffix ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Now you have a go! Someone wrote **"televition"**. What should it be?

interaction.question: What is the correct spelling?
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! The base is 'televise' which ends in S, so it's -sion: television. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! The base is 'televise' which ends in S, so it's -sion: television.

screens.title: -tion or -sion? The quick check!
screens.body: You've got a really useful trick now. Here's how to choose the right ending every time:

steps.text: Step 1: Look at the letter BEFORE the ending
steps.why: What letter does the base word end with?

steps.text: Step 2: Is it L, N, R or S?
steps.why: If YES → use -sion (e.g. expuLsion, teNsion, veRsion, expresSion)

steps.text: Step 3: Is it any other letter?
steps.why: If YES → use -tion (e.g. educaTion, invenTion, celebraTion)

steps.text: Still unsure? Say the base word out loud
steps.why: If it ends with a 'zh' or 'sh' sound, it's probably -sion ✓

learningGoal: How to recognise when letters are in disguise, making unexpected sounds
How to spot the common patterns — soft c, soft g, ph=f, ch=k — so they never trick you

screens.title: Letters in disguise!
screens.body: Did you know that English letters love dressing up as other letters? Daisy is learning about soft C and has just discovered that the letter **c** can sound like **s (as in city)**! How sneaky is that?

visualProps.text: The letter "c" can make the sound "s (as in city)"!
visualProps.label: Unexpected sound:

screens.title: When c makes a different sound
screens.body: On the previous screen, we saw how **"city"** uses the letter **c** to make the sound **s (as in city)**. C makes an S sound before E, I, or Y.

steps.text: c usually sounds like: k (as in cat)
steps.why: The sound you'd expect

steps.text: But sometimes it sounds like: s (as in city)
steps.why: C makes an S sound before E, I, or Y.

steps.text: Examples: city, ceiling, cycle, cent, circle, cinema
steps.why: Learn these as a group

steps.text: The spelling uses the LETTER, not the sound
steps.why: Don't write what you hear — write the correct letters ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Now it's your turn! Which of these is spelled correctly?

interaction.question: Which spelling is correct?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! Even though it sounds like 'sircle', it's spelled with a C because C makes an S sound before I. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Even though it sounds like 'sircle', it's spelled with a C because C makes an S sound before I.

screens.title: Letters in disguise — summary
screens.body: These patterns trip people up because the spelling doesn't match the sound. But once you know the disguises, they can't fool you any more!

steps.text: Soft C → S sound before E, I, Y
steps.why: city, ceiling, cycle, cent, circle

steps.text: Soft G → J sound before E, I, Y
steps.why: gym, gentle, giant, giraffe, energy
phone, photo, pharmacy, elephant
character, school, chemistry, stomach ✓

learningGoal: How to spot phonetic (spelled-by-sound) misspellings — English is full of them!
Why spelling by sound doesn't always work, and what to do instead

screens.title: Can you spot Tom's mistake?
screens.body: Tom wrote in his book and wrote **"fone"**. Here's the tricky thing — it sounds right when you say it, but the spelling is wrong! English loves to play tricks like this. Can you see why?

screens.title: Spelled by sound — but English had other plans!
screens.body: Tom wrote the F sound as 'f', but this word uses PH for the F sound. It comes from Greek.

steps.why: Tom wrote the F sound as 'f', but this word uses PH for the F sound. It comes from Greek.

steps.text: In English, the same sound can be spelled different ways
steps.why: You have to learn which letters each word uses
The correct spelling uses the right letter pattern ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Time to test your skills! Someone wrote **"foto"**. Can you fix it?

interaction.question: What is the correct spelling?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! The F sound in 'photo' is spelled PH, not F. It comes from the Greek word for light. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! The F sound in 'photo' is spelled PH, not F. It comes from the Greek word for light.

screens.title: Don't spell by sound alone!
screens.body: English has borrowed words from so many languages that the spelling often doesn't match the sound. But now you know the secret patterns, and that puts you way ahead!

steps.text: F sound might be spelled: f, ph, or gh (laugh)
steps.why: phone, not fone; photo, not foto

steps.text: J sound might be spelled: j, g, or dge (badge)
steps.why: gym, not jym; giant, not jiant

steps.text: K sound might be spelled: k, c, ck, or ch
steps.why: character, not karacter; school, not skool

steps.text: S sound might be spelled: s, c, or ss
steps.why: city, not sity; circle, not sircle ✓

subConcepts.name: Words You Just Have to Learn

learningGoal: How to use clever memory tricks (mnemonics) for the hardest common words — these are really fun!
How to break tricky words into parts so your brain locks them in

screens.title: How do you spell "because"?
screens.body: Did you know that **"because"** is one of the most misspelled words in the whole English language? Isla is learning to spell 'because'. This word doesn't follow the usual rules — but there's a brilliant trick to help you remember it!

screens.title: Memory trick for "because"
screens.body: Take the first letter of each word in the sentence to spell B-E-C-A-U-S-E.

steps.why: Break it down: be-cause

steps.text: Memory trick: Big Elephants Can Always Understand Small Elephants
steps.why: Take the first letter of each word in the sentence to spell B-E-C-A-U-S-E.
Now you know the trick, you won't make this mistake!
Writing it helps your hand remember the pattern ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Right, you've got this! One of these is the correct spelling — can you pick it?

interaction.question: Which is the correct spelling?
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! Remember: Big Elephants Can Always Understand Small Elephants → B-E-C-A-U-S-E. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Remember: Big Elephants Can Always Understand Small Elephants → B-E-C-A-U-S-E.

screens.title: Memory tricks for tricky words
screens.body: Some words just don't follow the rules — but that's OK! Use **mnemonics** (memory tricks) to outsmart them. These tricks are so good, you'll never forget them!

steps.text: because → Big Elephants Can Always Understand Small Elephants
steps.why: First letters spell B-E-C-A-U-S-E

steps.text: separate → there's A RAT in sepARATE
steps.why: Look for hidden words inside tricky words
Pronounce the silent letters when practising

steps.text: definitely → it has the word FINITE inside
steps.why: de-FINITE-ly — no A anywhere! ✓

learningGoal: How to spot the most common misspellings of tricky words — you'll be surprised how often you see them!
How to use memory tricks to avoid these mistakes and impress your teacher

screens.title: Can you spot Finn's mistake?
screens.body: Finn wrote in his diary and wrote **"definately"**. It looks close, but it's not quite right! Don't worry — this is one of the words that almost everybody gets wrong.

screens.title: Here's what went wrong
screens.body: Finn wrote 'definately' with an A, but the correct spelling has no A. Think of the word FINITE inside: de-FINITE-ly. Don't worry — now you know the trick, you'll remember it for life!

steps.why: Finn wrote 'definately' with an A, but the correct spelling has no A. Think of the word FINITE inside: de-FINITE-ly.

steps.text: Memory trick: de-FINITE-ly — the word FINITE is inside
steps.why: Use this to remember the correct spelling
Now you'll get this right every time ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Someone wrote **"goverment"**. What should it be?

interaction.question: What is the correct spelling?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! It's govern-ment — the word 'govern' keeps all its letters when you add -ment. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! It's govern-ment — the word 'govern' keeps all its letters when you add -ment.

screens.title: Spelling demons — learn them by heart
screens.body: These words trip up almost everyone — but not you any more! Use the tricks below, practise writing them out, and you'll have them nailed in no time.

steps.why: The most commonly misspelled word in English
Say all the syllables: Feb-roo-ary
Say: LIB-rare-ee
The word 'govern' stays complete ✓

learningGoal: How to remember that adding a prefix (a letter group at the start, like un- or re-) never changes the base word spelling — this is a golden rule!
How to recognise when prefixes naturally create double letters (and why that's perfectly fine)

screens.title: Why does "disappear" have a double letter?
screens.body: Aisha is checking her English homework and needs to spell **"disappear"**. Lots of people write **"disapear"** — but that's wrong! Here's the golden rule that makes it easy: **adding a prefix never changes the base word**.

screens.title: The golden rule: don't change the base word
screens.body: This rule is beautifully simple: when you add a prefix, keep the base word exactly as it is. No exceptions! The prefix 'dis-' is added to 'appear'. The base word doesn't change, so both letters stay.

interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! Write the base word first, add the prefix, and never drop a letter. You've got it! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Nearly there — always start with the base word, then add the prefix in front.

steps.why: Write this out first — don't change any letters
The prefix "dis" goes right before "appear"
The two P's come from the base word — 'appear' already has two P's.
Both parts keep their full spelling — that's the rule ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Right, you've got this! One of these is the correct spelling — can you pick it?

interaction.question: Which is the correct spelling?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Dis-' + 'agree' = 'disagree'. The base word 'agree' stays exactly the same. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 'Dis-' + 'agree' = 'disagree'. The base word 'agree' stays exactly the same.

screens.title: Prefix spelling recipe
screens.body: Here's the best bit: the base word **never changes** when you add a prefix. If this creates a double letter, that's perfectly correct! Don't be tempted to drop one.

steps.why: Don't drop a letter — keep both parts whole
One S from 'mis', one from 'spell'
One N from 'un', one from 'necessary'
One R from 'over', one from 'rule' ✓

learningGoal: How to spot when a prefix (beginning like un- or dis-) has wrongly changed the base word — a really common mistake!
How to fix prefix spelling mistakes quickly and confidently

screens.title: Can you spot Grace's mistake?
screens.body: Grace wrote a letter to her teacher and wrote **"disatisfied"**. Is this right, or has a letter been lost when adding the prefix?

screens.title: Check: is the base word still whole?
screens.body: Grace dropped the second S. 'Dis-' + 'satisfied' = 'dissatisfied' — the S from 'dis' and the S from 'satisfied' both stay. Here's your detective trick: always split the word into prefix + base and check both parts are complete.

steps.why: Split it into prefix + base word

steps.text: The base word must stay exactly the same
steps.why: Don't drop or change any letters
Both prefix and base word keep their full spelling ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Someone wrote **"imature"**. What should it be?

interaction.question: What is the correct spelling of "imature"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! 'Im-' + 'mature' = 'immature'. The M from 'im' and the M from 'mature' both stay. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! 'Im-' + 'mature' = 'immature'. The M from 'im' and the M from 'mature' both stay.

screens.title: How to check prefix spellings
screens.body: You've now got a foolproof checking method! When you see a word with a prefix, split it into two parts. If the base word has been changed, the spelling is wrong. Simple as that!

steps.text: Split the word: prefix + base word
steps.why: Find where the prefix ends and the base word starts

steps.text: Check: is the base word still whole?
steps.why: Every letter must still be there
dis + satisfied = dissatisfied (two S's is correct)
Never drop a letter to avoid a double ✓

learningGoal: How to decide between -able and -ible endings — there's a surprisingly simple test!
How to use the 'complete word test' to choose the right ending with confidence

screens.title: Is it -able or -ible?
screens.body: This is one of the trickiest spelling dilemmas in the English language! Ella is writing about a comfortable sofa in her story. The word is **"comfortable"**. But is it -able or -ible? There's a clever test that makes it surprisingly easy!

screens.title: The complete word test
screens.body: The word **"comfortable"** uses **-able**. How do you know? Remove the ending — is what's left a complete English word? If yes → **-able**. If no → **-ible**.

steps.why: Let's remove the ending and see what's left
"comfort" IS a complete word ✓
'Comfort' is a complete English word on its own, so we add '-able'.
The ending is "-able" ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Time to use the complete word test yourself! Is it **-able** or **-ible**? Can you pick the correct spelling?

interaction.question: Which is the correct spelling of "enjoyable"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'Enjoy' is a complete word, so we add '-able' → 'enjoyable'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 'Enjoy' is a complete word, so we add '-able' → 'enjoyable'.

screens.title: -able vs -ible cheat sheet
screens.body: You've got a brilliant shortcut now! Use the complete word test: if the base is a real word → **-able**. If not → **-ible**. This works most of the time and puts you ahead of the game.

steps.why: -able is more common — use it when the base word stands alone

steps.text: -ible (base is NOT a word): poss→possible, vis→visible
steps.why: -ible words usually come from Latin roots

steps.text: Watch out: notice → noticeable (keep the E!)
steps.why: Some -able words keep the E to protect a soft C or G sound

steps.text: When in doubt, -able is the safer guess
steps.why: About 900 words use -able but only about 200 use -ible ✓

learningGoal: How to spot -able/-ible mistakes in writing — they're hiding everywhere once you start looking!
How to apply the complete word test to fix errors like a spelling expert

screens.title: Can you spot Ben's mistake?
screens.body: Ben wrote in his book review and wrote **"incredable"**. Has the wrong ending been used? Let's put on our detective hats and find out!

screens.title: Apply the complete word test
screens.body: Ben wrote '-able' but 'incred' isn't a complete word, so it should be '-ible' → 'incredible'.

steps.why: Let's test: remove the ending and check the base
"incred" IS a complete word → -able
The correct ending gives us "incredible" ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Someone wrote **"horrable"**. What should it be?

interaction.question: What is the correct spelling of "horrable"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Fantastic! 'Horr' isn't a complete word, so we use '-ible' → 'horrible'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! 'Horr' isn't a complete word, so we use '-ible' → 'horrible'.

screens.title: Remember the test!
screens.body: Whenever you're stuck on -able or -ible, you've got your trusty test: remove the ending and check the base word. It works nearly every time — you've got this!

steps.why: What's left?

steps.text: Is the base a complete English word?
steps.why: Yes → -able / No → -ible

steps.text: Soft C or G? Keep the E!
steps.why: noticeable, changeable, manageable
It's correct about 4 times out of 5 ✓

learningGoal: How to form plurals (the spelling for more than one) for words ending in -y, -f/-fe, -s/-x/-ch/-sh — not everything just adds an S!
How to remember common irregular plurals that love to break the rules

screens.title: More than one city — but how do you spell it?
screens.body: Daisy is writing about her holiday visiting lots of cities and needs the plural of **"city"**. Is it **"citys"**? Nope! Did you know that some plurals are much trickier than just adding -s? Let's find out why.

visualProps.label: How do we make it plural?

screens.title: The "consonant + y" plural rule
screens.body: 'City' ends in consonant + Y. Change the Y to I and add -es: city → cities. Let's see how this works step by step:

interaction.sentence: To make "city" plural, we ____ to get "cities"
options: change Y to I and add -es
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! 'City' ends in consonant + Y. Change the Y to I and add -es: city → cities. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — 'City' ends in consonant + Y. Change the Y to I and add -es: city → cities.

steps.why: 'City' ends in consonant + Y. Change the Y to I and add -es: city → cities.
NOT "citys" — that's wrong!
baby → babies, story → stories, puppy → puppies

steps.text: The pattern is always the same
steps.why: Learn the rule and it works every time ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Let's see if you've got it! What is the plural of **"party"**?

interaction.question: What is the correct plural of "party"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Party' ends in consonant + Y → change Y to I and add -es → 'parties'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 'Party' ends in consonant + Y → change Y to I and add -es → 'parties'.

screens.title: Plural rules cheat sheet
screens.body: Most plurals just add -s — but the tricky ones love to catch people out! Here are the patterns to remember:

steps.text: Consonant + Y → change Y to I, add -es
steps.why: city→cities, party→parties, baby→babies
leaf→leaves, knife→knives, wolf→wolves
bus→buses, fox→foxes, church→churches
child→children, mouse→mice, tooth→teeth, person→people ✓

learningGoal: How to spot incorrect plural spellings that crop up all the time in everyday writing
How to apply the right plural rule to fix mistakes confidently

screens.title: Can you spot Grace's mistake?
screens.body: Grace wrote a story about wildlife and wrote **"wolfs"** as the plural of **"wolf"**. That doesn't look right! This is a really common mistake — can you see why it's wrong?

screens.title: Here's the correct plural
screens.body: Grace wrote 'wolfs' but 'wolf' ends in F. Change the F to V and add -es → 'wolves'.

steps.why: "wolf" doesn't just add -s
Grace wrote 'wolfs' but 'wolf' ends in F. Change the F to V and add -es → 'wolves'.
wolf→wolves, leaf→leaves, shelf→shelves, knife→knives, calf→calves

steps.text: The pattern: change F to V, then add -es
steps.why: This works for most words ending in F or FE ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Someone wrote **"halfs"**. What should it be?

interaction.question: What is the correct plural?
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! 'Half' ends in F → change to V, add -es → 'halves'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! 'Half' ends in F → change to V, add -es → 'halves'.

screens.title: Plural mistake checklist
screens.body: Before writing a plural, take a quick look at the ending of the singular word — it tells you exactly what to do:

steps.text: Ends in consonant + Y? → Change Y to I, add -es
steps.why: city→cities, story→stories (but monkey→monkeys — vowel + Y!)
wolf→wolves, wife→wives (but roof→roofs!)
fox→foxes, wish→wishes, bus→buses
child→children, foot→feet, goose→geese ✓

subConcepts.name: Practice or Practise? Advice or Advise?

learningGoal: Some words sneakily change their spelling depending on whether they are a thing (noun) or an action (verb) — like practice/practise and advice/advise
A brilliantly simple trick to remember: C is for things, S is for actions

screens.title: C or S? Which spelling do you need?
screens.body: Did you know that in British English, the same word can be spelled differently depending on how you use it? Charlie is writing about football after school and needs to choose: **"I need more _____ before the match on Saturday."** Is it **practice** (with a C) or **practise** (with an S)?

screens.title: C for things, S for actions!
screens.body: Is it **"practice"** (with C) or **"practise"** (with S)? This catches out grown-ups all the time, but the rule is actually really simple:

interaction.sentence: In British English, nouns use ____ and verbs use S (e.g. advice vs advise)
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Nouns use C (like "ice" — a thing) and verbs use S (like "advise" — an action). ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — nouns use C (practice, advice, licence) and verbs use S!

steps.text: If the word is a THING (noun) → spell it with C
steps.why: adviCe (a thing you give), praCtiCe (a thing you do), liCenCe (a card you hold)

steps.text: If the word is an ACTION (verb) → spell it with S
steps.why: adviSe (to tell someone), practiSe (to do something), licenSe (to allow)

steps.text: Read the sentence: "I need more _____ before the match on Saturday."
steps.why: Here we need the noun — 'more practice' is a thing. Nouns use C (like 'ice' — a thing).

steps.text: Answer: "I need more practice before the match on Saturday."
steps.why: It's a thing (noun) → C ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Nearly there! Pick the correct word for this sentence.

interaction.question: Which word correctly completes: "The team _____ every Tuesday and Thursday."
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! Here 'practise' is a verb — the team does an action. Verbs use S. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Here 'practise' is a verb — the team does an action. Verbs use S.

visualProps.text: The team _____ every Tuesday and Thursday.
visualProps.label: Fill in the correct word:

screens.title: The C/S rule
screens.body: You've just learned something that most adults don't even know! In British English, these word pairs use **C for the noun** and **S for the verb**. Remember: **ice** is a thing, **advise** is an action.

steps.why: 'Football practice' (thing) vs 'I practise piano' (action)
'Give me advice' (thing) vs 'I advise you' (action)
'A driving licence' (thing) vs 'licensed to sell' (action)

steps.text: Memory trick: iCe = noun (C), adviSe = verb (S)
steps.why: C for the thing, S for the doing ✓

learningGoal: How to spot C/S spelling errors in sentences — you'll find them everywhere once you know the trick!
How to tell nouns (naming words) from verbs (doing words) in context

screens.title: Can you spot Aisha's mistake?
screens.body: Aisha wrote in her diary and wrote: **"Mum always gives me good advise about school."** One of the C/S words is wrong! Can you find it?

visualProps.text: Mum always gives me good advise about school.
visualProps.label: Spot the mistake:

screens.title: Noun or verb? C or S?
screens.body:
'Good advise' is wrong because this is a noun — a thing Mum gives. Nouns use C → 'advice'.

**Your C or S quick check:**
1. Find the word (practice/advice/licence)
2. Is it a thing (noun) or an action (verb)? Try: can you say "the ___"? → noun
3. **Noun → C** (advi**c**e = thing), **Verb → S** (advi**s**e = action)
4. This only applies in British English!

steps.why: Is this word a noun (thing) or verb (action)?

steps.text: In this sentence, it's a noun
steps.why: 'Good advise' is wrong because this is a noun — a thing Mum gives. Nouns use C → 'advice'.
Nouns use C ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Time to put your skills to the test! Look at this sentence: **"I practise the piano every day after school."** Is the underlined word correct?

interaction.question: Is "practise" correct in: "I practise the piano every day after school."
interaction.correctFeedback: Excellent! 'Practise' here is a verb — an action. Verbs use S, so 'practise' is correct! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 'Practise' here is a verb — an action. Verbs use S, so 'practise' is correct!

visualProps.text: I practise the piano every day after school.
visualProps.label: Fill in the correct word:

screens.title: C or S — the quick check
screens.body: All you need to remember is one question: is it a thing or an action? That's it!

steps.text: Step 1: Is the word a THING (noun)?
steps.why: If yes → spell it with C

steps.text: Step 2: Is the word an ACTION (verb)?
steps.why: If yes → spell it with S
I gave some advice. I advise you to study.
Piano practice. I practise every day.
A driving licence. Licensed to drive. ✓

learningGoal: How to spot unstressed vowels that love to hide — these cause some of the most common spelling mistakes!
How to use syllable-splitting (breaking a word into its beats, like def-i-nite-ly) to catch the hidden vowel

screens.title: The vowel you can't hear!
screens.body: Did you know your mouth secretly swallows some vowels when you speak quickly? Ella is writing about what she definitely wants for her birthday and wrote **"definately"**. It looks almost right — but a sneaky vowel has been swapped or lost!

screens.title: Split it into syllables
screens.body: The sneaky vowel is **i** in the third syllable: def-i-nite-ly. Here's how to find it:

steps.text: Say the word SLOWLY: "def · i · nite · ly"
steps.why: Break it into syllables and say each one clearly
It's in the third syllable: def-i-nite-ly

steps.text: Memory trick: Think of the word 'FINITE' hiding inside: de-FINITE-ly
steps.why: A trick to remember the tricky letter
Now you know where the sneaky vowel hides ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Right, you've got this! One of these is the correct spelling — can you pick it?

interaction.question: Which is the correct spelling?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Say each syllable: sep-a-rate-ly. There's an A in the middle, not an E. Think: 'there's A RAT in separately!' ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Say each syllable: sep-a-rate-ly. There's an A in the middle, not an E. Think: 'there's A RAT in separately!'

screens.title: How to catch sneaky vowels
screens.body: Unstressed vowels all sound the same when we speak quickly — like a lazy "uh". But they're different letters in the spelling! Slow down, and you'll catch them every time.

steps.why: Think: de-FINITE-ly — 'finite' is hiding inside
Think: there's A RAT in separately
Think: it starts with BUS

steps.text: Say every syllable slowly and clearly
steps.why: The hidden vowel reveals itself when you slow down ✓

learningGoal: How to spot unstressed vowel errors in writing — once you know what to look for, they pop out at you!
How to use syllable-splitting and memory tricks to fix them for good

screens.title: Can you spot Daisy's mistake?
screens.body: Daisy wrote in her English essay and wrote **"seperate"**. A sneaky vowel has gone wrong — but don't worry, this is one of the most common mistakes in English! Can you find the error?

screens.title: Split it into syllables to find the mistake
screens.body: Daisy wrote 'seperate' but the hidden vowel is A, not E. Remember: there's A RAT in 'separate'!

steps.why: The sneaky vowel is: a

steps.text: Say it slowly, syllable by syllable
steps.why: Daisy wrote 'seperate' but the hidden vowel is A, not E. Remember: there's A RAT in 'separate'!
The correct vowel is restored ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Someone wrote **"differant"**. What should it be?

interaction.question: What is the correct spelling of "differant"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! Say it slowly: dif-fer-ENT. The last syllable is '-ent', not '-ant'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! Say it slowly: dif-fer-ENT. The last syllable is '-ent', not '-ant'.

screens.title: Unstressed vowel survival guide
screens.body: These are some of the most commonly misspelled words in the English language — even adults struggle with them! But now you've got syllable-splitting and memory tricks, you can beat them all.

steps.text: Say every syllable clearly and slowly
steps.why: The hidden vowel appears when you slow down

steps.text: Learn memory tricks for the worst ones
steps.why: de-FINITE-ly, A RAT in separate, BUS-i-ness

steps.text: Write the tricky word out 5 times
steps.why: Your hand learns the spelling even when your ear can't hear it

steps.text: When in doubt, look it up!
steps.why: There's no shame in checking — even adults get these wrong ✓

learningGoal: How to apply the three suffix (word ending) rules: drop the E, double the consonant, change Y to I — three rules that cover almost everything!
How to decide which rule to use so you get it right first time

screens.title: What happens when you add "-ing"?
screens.body: Here's a spelling puzzle! Oliver is writing about hoping to win the race. Adding **"-ing"** to **"hope"** — is it **"hopeing"**? Nope! There's a handy rule that tells you exactly what to do.

screens.title: Rule: drop the silent E
screens.body: 'Hope' ends in a silent E. When adding a vowel suffix (-ing), drop the E: hope → hoping.

steps.why: Look at the ending of the base word
'Hope' ends in a silent E. When adding a vowel suffix (-ing), drop the E: hope → hoping.
Apply the rule before adding the suffix
But before a consonant suffix (-ful), we KEEP the E: hope → hopeful. ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: You've got this! What is **"make"** + **"-ing"**?

interaction.question: What is "make" + "-ing"?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Make' ends in silent E. Drop the E before '-ing' → 'making'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 'Make' ends in silent E. Drop the E before '-ing' → 'making'.

screens.title: Three suffix rules to remember
screens.body: You've now got three powerful rules in your toolkit! Before adding a suffix, just check the base word ending and pick the right one:

steps.text: Silent E? Drop it before vowel suffixes
steps.why: hope→hoping, make→making (but hopeful — keep E before consonant)

steps.text: Short vowel + single consonant? Double it
steps.why: run→running, sit→sitting, begin→beginning

steps.text: Consonant + Y? Change Y to I
steps.why: happy→happiness, beauty→beautiful (but carry→carrying — keep Y before -ing)

steps.text: Ask: what's the last letter? What's the suffix?
steps.why: The ending of the base word tells you which rule to use ✓

learningGoal: How to spot suffix-adding (word ending) errors in writing — these pop up all the time!
How to apply the correct rule to fix the mistake quickly and confidently

screens.title: Can you spot Isla's mistake?
screens.body: Isla wrote in her story about a race and wrote **"runing"**. A suffix rule has been broken — can you spot which one? This is a really common slip-up!

screens.title: Here's the rule that was broken
screens.body: Isla forgot to double the N. 'Run' has a short vowel + single consonant, so we double: run → running.

steps.why: Base word: "run" + suffix: "-ing"

steps.text: The rule: Isla forgot to double the N. 'Run' has a short vowel + single consonant, so we double: run → running.
steps.why: Check the base word ending, then apply the rule
Now the suffix rule is followed correctly ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Someone wrote **"stoping"**. What should it be?

interaction.question: What is the correct spelling?
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! 'Stop' has a short vowel (o) + single consonant (p). Double the P → 'stopping'. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Close! 'Stop' has a short vowel (o) + single consonant (p). Double the P → 'stopping'.

screens.title: Suffix rules — quick checklist
screens.body: You're now a suffix expert! Before adding a suffix, just run through this quick checklist:

steps.text: Silent E + vowel suffix → drop the E
steps.why: hope→hoping, excite→exciting, write→writing

steps.text: Short vowel + single consonant → double it
steps.why: run→running, stop→stopping, begin→beginning

steps.text: Consonant + Y + suffix → change Y to I
steps.why: happy→happiness, lazy→lazier, beauty→beautiful
carry→carrying, study→studying, play→playing ✓
